sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.content:
Persistent HPV infection: Persistent HPV infection is the most important factor in the development of cervical cancer. High-risk HPV types 16 and 18 are most commonly implicated, found in the majority of HPV-associated cervical SCCs. In countries with a high incidence of cervical cancer, the prevalence of chronic HPV is approximately 10% to 20%, whereas in low-incidence countries it is 5% to 10%. Strength: Primary etiologic factor — approximately 95% of cervical cancers are HPV-associated [MS-2, CERV-A 1 of 7].
Smoking: A history of smoking is an epidemiologic risk factor associated with cervical cancer. Smoking cessation should be advised in current smokers, and former smokers should continue to avoid smoking. Strength: Established risk factor [MS-2, MS-3].
Oral contraceptive use: Oral contraceptive use has been identified as an epidemiologic risk factor associated with cervical cancer. Strength: Established risk factor [MS-2].
Early age of onset of coitus: Early age of onset of sexual intercourse is an epidemiologic risk factor associated with cervical cancer. Strength: Established risk factor [MS-2].
Larger number of sexual partners: A larger number of sexual partners is an epidemiologic risk factor associated with cervical cancer. Strength: Established risk factor [MS-2].
History of sexually transmitted disease: A history of sexually transmitted disease is an epidemiologic risk factor associated with cervical cancer. Strength: Established risk factor [MS-2].
Certain autoimmune diseases: Certain autoimmune diseases have been identified as epidemiologic risk factors associated with cervical cancer. Strength: Established risk factor [MS-2].
Chronic immunosuppression: Chronic immunosuppression, including HIV infection, is an epidemiologic risk factor associated with cervical cancer. The guidelines recommend considering HIV testing, especially in younger patients. Patients with cervical cancer and HIV should be referred to an HIV specialist and should be treated for cervical cancer as per the guidelines without modification solely on the basis of HIV status. Strength: Established risk factor [MS-2, CERV-1 footnote d].
